source: Hengl, T. (2018). Global Landform and Lithology / USGS EcoTapestry

classes.name: Acid Plutonics
classes.description: Felsic intrusive igneous rocks (for example granite and granodiorite) formed by slow cooling at depth. These terrains are typically hard, fractured, and nutrient-poor, with sandy to coarse weathering products.

classes.name: Acid Volcanics
classes.description: Felsic extrusive rocks (for example rhyolite and obsidian) produced by silica-rich volcanism. They are commonly tied to explosive eruptions and often weather into stony, shallow, and patchy soils.

classes.name: Basic Plutonics
classes.description: Mafic intrusive rocks (for example gabbro and norite) crystallized slowly below the surface. Their weathering tends to produce darker, base-rich materials and comparatively fertile substrates.

classes.name: Basic Volcanics
classes.description: Mafic volcanic rocks (mainly basaltic to andesitic) erupted at the surface. They are widespread in lava plateaus and oceanic settings, and can weather into clay-rich, relatively nutrient-rich soils.

classes.name: Carbonate Sedimentary Rock
classes.description: Carbonate strata (limestone, dolostone, chalk) formed by biogenic accumulation or chemical precipitation. Commonly linked to karst, sinkholes, caves, and alkaline groundwater chemistry.

classes.name: Evaporite
classes.description: Highly soluble chemical sediments (halite, gypsum, anhydrite) precipitated from evaporating saline waters. Typical of arid basins and often associated with salinity stress and unstable ground conditions.

classes.name: Ice and Glaciers
classes.description: Permanent or long-lived glacial ice cover where bedrock is largely obscured. Surface processes are cryogenic, with sparse soil development and strong controls from freeze-thaw and meltwater routing.

classes.name: Intermediate Plutonics
classes.description: Intermediate-composition intrusive rocks (for example diorite and tonalite) bridging felsic and mafic endmembers. They generally weather to mixed mineral soils with moderate fertility and variable texture.

classes.name: Intermediate Volcanics
classes.description: Intermediate volcanic rocks (notably andesite-dacite suites) common along convergent margins. They are often associated with stratovolcanoes, tephra deposits, and steep erosive terrain.

classes.name: Metamorphics
classes.description: Rocks transformed by heat, pressure, and deformation (for example schist, gneiss, quartzite, marble). These areas often show strong structural fabric, variable permeability, and rugged topography.

classes.name: Mixed Sedimentary Rock
classes.description: Heterogeneous sedimentary successions with interbedded carbonate, clastic, or chemical units where no single lithology dominates. Hydrology and soil properties can change sharply over short distances.

classes.name: Pyroclastics
classes.description: Fragmental volcanic deposits (for example ash tuff, lapilli tuff, ignimbrite, volcanic breccia) emplaced by explosive eruptions. They commonly produce porous, erosion-prone substrates and complex stratigraphy.

classes.name: Siliciclastic Sedimentary
classes.description: Detrital sedimentary rocks composed of silicate grains (sandstone, siltstone, mudstone, shale). They dominate many basins and plains, with permeability and erosion behavior controlled by grain size and cementation.

classes.name: Unconsolidated Sediment
classes.description: Loose surficial deposits that are not lithified (alluvium, colluvium, dune sand, till, loess). These materials are highly dynamic and strongly influence drainage, rooting depth, and disturbance sensitivity.

classes.name: Undefined
classes.description: Lithology data not available or not classified for this area.
